content.text:
Different rings have different structural properties. Understanding 
these distinctions—units, zero divisors, nilpotents—is essential for 
ring theory.

content.title: Unit
content.content:
An element $u$ of a ring $R$ with unity is a **unit** 
(or invertible element) if there exists $v \in R$ with $uv = vu = 1$. 
We write $v = u^{-1}$.

content.title: Group of Units
content.content:
The set of all units in a ring $R$ is denoted $R^*$ or $U(R)$. It forms 
a group under multiplication.

content.title: Units in various rings
content.problem:
- $\Z^* = \{1, -1\}$

- $\Q^* = \Q \setminus \{0\}$ (all nonzero rationals)

- $\Z_n^* = U(n)$ (integers coprime to $n$)

- $\Z[i]^* = \{1, -1, i, -i\}$

- $R[x]^* = R^*$ for any integral domain $R$ (only constant 
 polynomials are units)

content.title: Zero Divisors vs Units
content.content: In a ring with unity, a zero divisor cannot be a unit.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Suppose $a$ is both a unit and a zero divisor. Then $ab = 0$ for some 
$b \neq 0$. Multiplying by $a^{-1}$: $b = a^{-1}(ab) = a^{-1} \cdot 0 = 0$. 
Contradiction.

content.title: Integral Domain
content.content:
An **integral domain** is a commutative 
ring with unity $1 \neq 0$ that has no zero divisors.

content.title: Integral domains
content.problem:
- $\Z$ is an integral domain

- $\Z_p$ is an integral domain iff $p$ is prime

- $\Z[x]$ is an integral domain

- $\Z_6$ is **not** an integral domain ($2 \cdot 3 = 0$)

content.title: Cancellation in Integral Domains
content.content: In an integral domain, if $ab = ac$ and $a \neq 0$, then $b = c$.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
$ab = ac$ implies $a(b - c) = 0$. Since $a \neq 0$ and there are no 
zero divisors, $b - c = 0$, so $b = c$.

content.title: Nilpotent Element
content.content:
An element $a \in R$ is **nilpotent** if $a^n = 0$ 
for some positive integer $n$.

content.title: Idempotent Element
content.content:
An element $e \in R$ is **idempotent** if 
$e^2 = e$.

content.title: Nilpotent and idempotent elements
content.problem:
In $\Z_{12}$:

- Nilpotent: $6^2 = 36 \equiv 0$, so $6$ is nilpotent

- Idempotent: $4^2 = 16 \equiv 4$ and $9^2 = 81 \equiv 9$
The only idempotents in an integral domain are $0$ and $1$.

items:
Prove that $\Z[\sqrt{2}]$ has infinitely many units. (Hint: 
 Consider $(1 + \sqrt{2})^n$.)
Prove that the sum of a unit and a nilpotent is a unit.

Show that an integral domain with finitely many elements is a 
 field.

marginNotes.text:
A wife of noble character who can find? 
She is worth far more than rubies.
